lesson:
始はじめる is transitive — you start something. Its intransitive partner is 始はじまる (はじまる) — something starts on its own. 仕し事ごとを始はじめる (I start work). 映えい画がが始はじまる (the movie starts). This is a classic える/ある pair, and the pattern repeats: 終おえる/終おわる, 変かえる/変かわる, 決きめる/決きまる. Internalize this one pair and the others click into place.

Key pattern: 〜始はじめる attached to a verb’s masu-stem means “start doing.” 雨あめが降ふり始はじめた (it started raining). 読よみ始はじめる (start reading). The mirror of 〜終おわる. Together they bracket any action.

The kanji 始はじ combines 女おんな (woman) + 台 (platform). The connection traces to the idea of a woman as the origin of life — birth as the first beginning. It appears in 原げん始し (げんし, primitive) and 始し祖そ (しそ, founder).

Particles: 始はじめる takes を — the thing you start is your direct object. 新あたらしい生せい活を始はじめる (start a new life).

Potential が始はじめられる: が marks what can be started. 来く月つきから授じゅ業ぎょうが始はじめられる (classes can be started from next month).

Passive に始はじめられる: に marks who started it. 先さきに始はじめられた (they started before me).

Causative に始はじめさせる: に marks who you make start. 生せい徒とに練習を始はじめさせる (have the students start practice).

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 始まる (はじまる)
Dictionaryはじめるを
Negativeはじめないを
Politeはじめますを
Polite negはじめませんを
Pastはじめたを
Polite pastはじめましたを
て-formはじめてを
Volitionalはじめようを
Polite volはじめましょうを
Passiveはじめられるに
Causativeはじめさせるに／を
Caus-passはじめさせられるに
Potentialはじめられるが
Imperativeはじめろを
Imp なさいはじめなさいを
Cond ～ばはじめればを
Cond ～たらはじめたらを

meaning: to wake, get up; to occur
lesson:
起おきる has two lives: people wake up, and events happen. 朝あさ早く起おきる (wake up early). 事じ故こが起おきた (an accident occurred). The connection is vertical motion — your body rises from sleep, a situation rises into being. The subject tells you which meaning: a person wakes up, a phenomenon arises.

Three related verbs: 起おきる (intransitive — wake up, occur). 起おこる (おこる) (also intransitive but only “occur”). 起おこす (おこす) (transitive — wake someone up, cause something). 子こ供どもを起おこす (wake the kid up). 問もん題だいを起おこす (cause a problem). For “wake someone up,” speakers usually reach for 起おこす rather than the causative of 起おきる.

The kanji 起お: 走はし (run) + 己 (self). The self getting up and moving.

Particles: 起おきる takes が for the subject — the person who wakes or the event that occurs. 子こ供どもが起おきた (the kid woke up). No direct object — it's intransitive. For time, use に: 六ろく時じに起おきる (wake up at six).

Passive: not commonly used — waking up is something you do, not something done to you. For events, you'd say 起おこされた (the transitive causative) if someone caused it.

Causative を／に起おきさせる: making someone wake up. 子こ供どもを起おきさせる (make the kid get up). In practice, 起おこす (the transitive pair) is more natural for "wake someone up."

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: 起こす (おこす)

Negativeおきないが
Politeおきますが
Polite negおきませんが
Pastおきたが
Polite pastおきましたが
て-formおきてが
Volitionalおきようが
Polite volおきましょうが
Passiveおきられるが
Causativeおきさせるを／に
Caus-passおきさせられるに
Potentialおきられるが
Imperativeおきろが
Imp なさいおきなさいが
Cond ～ばおきればが
Cond ～たらおきたらが

In Japan, new beginnings don’t cluster around January. They cluster around April — the start of the school year, the fiscal year, new jobs, fresh starts. And April is 春. Cherry blossoms, graduation ceremonies, first days at work. 春休み (はるやすみ, spring break) isn’t a vacation in the middle of the year — it’s the transition between endings and new beginnings.

The word はる may be related to 張る (はる, to swell/stretch) — the season when buds swell and burst open. Some linguists connect it to 墾る (はる, to plow), since spring is when farmers begin working fields. Either etymology roots the word in physical expansion — things opening up after winter’s constriction.

The kanji 春 has 日 (sun) at the bottom and strokes on top that originally represented emerging plants. Sun warming the earth, making things push upward.

午ご前ぜん is the mirror of 午ご後ご: 午ご (noon) + 前まえ (before) = "before noon." Together with 午ご後ご, they split the day at its midpoint. Clean, symmetrical, borrowed wholesale from the Chinese system of dividing time by the zodiac hours.

In usage, 午ご前ぜん goes before the time: 午ご前ぜん十時とき (10 AM). But here's something worth noticing: Japanese people don't always say 午ご前ぜん for "morning" in casual speech. They're more likely to say 朝あさ (あさ) for early morning or just use context. 午ご前ぜん is the precise, clock-oriented word — schedules, appointments, news broadcasts. 朝あさ is the lived experience of morning — waking up, the commute, breakfast.

午ご前ぜん中ちゅう (ごぜんちゅう) means "during the morning" or "throughout the morning" and is extremely useful: 午ご前ぜん中ちゅうに届とどきます (it'll arrive sometime in the morning). The 中なか adds a sense of "somewhere within that span" — no exact time, just the general morning window.

別べつに。 One word. Delivered with a shrug. It means “not particularly,” “I don’t really care,” or “nothing special.” In anime and drama, 別べつに... followed by a trailing-off is the classic tsundere response — pretending not to care while clearly caring. In real life: 別べつにいい (it’s fine, whatever). 別べつに何なにもない (nothing in particular).

The core adjective means “separate, different.” As a な-adjective: 別べつな問もん題だい (a separate problem) or 別べつの話はなし (a different topic). Both 別べつな and 別べつの are correct — 別べつの is slightly more common. The kanji 別べつ has a blade (刂りっとう) on the right — cutting apart. It shows up in 別べつれる (わかれる, to part), 区く別べつ (くべつ, distinction), 特とく別べつ (とくべつ, special).

Dictionaryべつだ
Negativeべつじゃない
Pastべつだった
Past negべつじゃなかった
Adverbべつに
て-formべつで

どこ is the question member of the ko-so-a-do place series: ここ (here), そこ (there, near you), あそこ (over there), どこ (where?). The system is perfectly regular — swap the first syllable and the meaning changes. You already know the logic from これ/それ/あれ/どれ.

どこに行いくの？ (where are you going?). トイレはどこですか (where's the bathroom?). どこから来くましたか (where are you from?). The particle after どこ shapes the question: に asks where you're heading, で asks where something happens, から asks where something comes from.

Like all question words in Japanese, どこ shapeshifts with particles: どこか (somewhere), どこも (everywhere / nowhere, depending on positive or negative), どこでも (anywhere). The pattern is identical to いつか/いつも/いつでも — learn one question word's particle combinations and you've learned them all.

The reading is へや. Not ぶや, not べや — へや. Neither kanji’s standard reading produces this sound. Just memorize it as a unit. 部ぶ (section) + 屋や (roof, building). A sectioned-off space under a roof.

屋や is worth knowing on its own. It turns any product into the shop that sells it: 本ほん屋や (ほんや, bookstore), 花はな屋や (はなや, florist), パン屋や (bakery). Learn a product noun, slap 屋や on the end, get the store.

部へ屋や is the default word for any room. 部へ屋やに入はいる (enter a room). 部へ屋やを片付ける (tidy up). In sumo, 部へ屋や also means a wrestling stable — 相撲すもう部屋べや (すもうべや) — where wrestlers live and train together.

若者 (わかもの, young people) shows up in news articles whenever a journalist wants to discuss generational trends. 若手 (わかて) is the workplace word for someone junior, still rising. Both treat youth not as an age but as a stage — not yet fully developed, still proving yourself. That’s the core feeling of 若い: fresh, unfinished, with growth ahead.

若い usually describes people but stretches to anything fresh or unripe. 若い人 (a young person). 若い頃 (when I was young). 若く見える (look young) — a compliment in any culture, but especially effective in Japan.

The kanji 若 has the grass radical (艸) on top — young shoots, tender growth. In Buddhist terminology, 般若 (はんにゃ) uses 若 for a completely different meaning (wisdom) — a reminder that many kanji live entirely different lives in different contexts. testtest

The word くるま likely comes from くる (to spin) + ま (round thing) — the spinning round thing. A wheel. Then the thing with wheels. Then the car. It’s a rare case where you can trace a modern Japanese word back to its physical origin and see the logic at every step.

The kanji 車くるま matches: a top-down view of a cart with the axle running through the middle, two wheels on either side. Oracle bone versions from 3,500 years ago show a two-wheeled chariot so clearly you could build one from the drawing.

In modern Japanese, 車くるま defaults to “car.” 車くるまで行いく (go by car). 車くるまを運うん転てんする (drive). But the older meaning survives in compounds: 電でん車しゃ (でんしゃ, train), 自じ転てん車しゃ (じてんしゃ, bicycle — “self-rotating vehicle”), 風ふう車しゃ (ふうしゃ, windmill).

〜ておく (often contracted to 〜とく in casual speech) means to do something in advance. 買かっておく (buy ahead of time). 調ちょうべておく (look it up beforehand). そのままにしておく (leave it as it is). The image: placing an action down for later use. This grammar pattern alone makes 置おく one of the most important verbs you’ll learn.

On its own, 置おく means to put or place something deliberately. テーブルに置おく (put on the table). 鍵かぎをどこに置おいた？ (where did I put the keys?). The particle に marks location, を marks the thing. The kanji 置お has 网あみがしら (net) over 直なお (straight) — setting something down in a fixed position.

Particles: 置おく takes を for the thing placed and に for the location. 荷に物もつをここに置おく (put the luggage here).

Potential が置おける: が marks what can be placed. ここに車くるまが置おける (you can park a car here).

Passive に置おかれる: に marks the person who placed it. 誰だれかに変かな場ば所しょに置おかれた (it was put in a weird spot by someone).

Causative に…を置おかせる: に marks who you make put something down. 子こ供どもに荷に物もつを置おかせる (have the kid put down the bags).

五段 Godan他動詞

Negativeおかないを
Politeおきますを
Polite negおきませんを
Pastおいたを
Polite pastおきましたを
て-formおいてを
Volitionalおこうを
Polite volおきましょうを
Passiveおかれるに
Causativeおかせるに／を
Caus-passおかせられるに
Potentialおけるが
Imperativeおけを
Imp なさいおきなさいを
Cond ～ばおけばを
Cond ～たらおいたらを

You’ll almost never use 住すむ in its dictionary form. Once you live somewhere, you’re in the state of living there. So Japanese uses 住んでいる: 東ひがし京に住すんでいる (I live in Tokyo). This mirrors 知しる — the dictionary form describes the moment of transition, the ている form describes the resulting state. If someone asks where you live, the answer is always 〜に住すんでいます.

The kanji 住 is 亻にんべん (person) + 主 (master). A person who is the master of a place — settled, rooted. 住すむ is about where your life is anchored. Not being alive (生うきる), not temporarily staying (泊まるとまる).

住す feeds into useful compounds: 住所じゅうしょ (じゅうしょ, address), 住す民 (じゅうみん, resident), 住す宅 (じゅうたく, housing). The on’yomi じゅう appears in formal contexts. The kun’yomi すむ stays in everyday speech.

Particles: 住すむ takes に — the place where you live. 日にち本ほんに住すむ (live in Japan). に marks the location of residence, the place you're rooted to.

Passive に住すまれる: uncommon, but possible in indirect passive — 隣となりに変かな人ひとに住すまれて困こまっている (a weird person lives next door and it's a problem for me).

Causative を／に住すませる: making or letting someone live somewhere. に for permissive — 学がく生うにこのアパートに住すませる (let students live in this apartment).

五段 Godan自動詞

Pastすんだ

て-formすんで

働はたら is rare among kanji — it was invented in Japan, not imported from China. It's a 国こく字じ (こくじ, national character), combining 亻にんべん (person) + 動どう (move). A person in motion. That's the Japanese definition of work: a human being moving, doing, physically engaging with the world. The Chinese character for work (工) is abstract — a beam or tool. The Japanese invention is embodied — it's about the worker, not the work.

働はたらく is intransitive — you work, but you don't "work" an object. The place where you work takes で: 会かい社しゃで働はたらく (work at a company). The field takes で too: IT業ぎょう界かいで働はたらいている (I work in the IT industry). Like 住すむ, you'll most often use the ている form to describe your ongoing work situation.

働はたらく can also mean "to function" or "to operate" for non-humans: 頭あたまが働はたらかない (my brain isn't working). 薬くすりが働はたらく (the medicine takes effect). And in a darker sense: 悪あく事じを働はたらく (commit a crime) — here 働はたらく unusually takes を, treating the wrongdoing as the object of labor.

Particles: 働はたらく takes で for the workplace — 病院びょういんで働はたらく (work at a hospital). で marks where the action happens. For the company or organization specifically, に also works: トヨタに働はたらいている.

Passive に働はたらかれる: indirect passive — someone working affects you. Rare in natural speech.

Causative を／に働はたらかせる: making someone work. を for coercive — 社しゃ員を休やすみなく働はたらかせる (make employees work without days off). A word you see in discussions about labor rights.

五段 Godan自動詞

て-formはたらいてで

Passiveはたらかれるに
Causativeはたらかせるを／に
Caus-passはたらかせられるに
Potentialはたらけるで
Imperativeはたらけで
Imp なさいはたらきなさいで
Cond ～ばはたらけばで
Cond ～たらはたらいたらで

難しいむずかしい顔かおをする — “to make a difficult face.” It means to look troubled or stern, and it reveals something about the word: むずかしい isn’t just about hard math. It can describe people who are hard to deal with — picky, demanding, socially complicated. あの人ひとは難しい means “that person is difficult” the way English might say “high-maintenance.”

For its primary meaning, 難しいむずかしい describes intellectual or situational complexity. 日本ほん語にほんごは難しい (Japanese is hard). It’s about things that challenge understanding, not things that exhaust you — for that, use 大変かたいへん (たいへん). A puzzle is 難しいむずかしい. Moving to a new house while working full-time is 大変かたいへん. One challenges your brain. The other exhausts your everything.

The kanji 難なん originally meant hardship or disaster. 隹ふるとり (short-tailed bird) plus a complex left component. The character still carries both senses: 災難なん (さいなん, disaster). Difficulty and suffering share a kanji.

Dictionaryむずかしい
Negativeむずかしくない
Pastむずかしかった
Past negむずかしくなかった
Adverbむずかしく
て-formむずかしくて

Your doctor is a 先せん生せい. Your lawyer is a 先せん生せい. The manga artist whose work you follow is a 先せん生せい. Politicians get called 先せん生せい. The word extends far beyond classroom teachers — it honors anyone with knowledge or authority that came from getting there first. The literal meaning tells you why: 先さき (before, ahead) + 生せい (born, life) = someone born before you, someone who arrived first.

In practice, 先せん生せい functions both as a noun (“a teacher”) and as a title. When used as a title, it replaces さん — you don’t say 田た中なか先せん生せいさん. And you can address someone directly as just 先せん生せい, the way English uses “Doctor” or “Professor” standalone.

There’s an ironic usage too. Sarcastically calling someone 先せん生せい implies they’re being arrogant or self-important. The same word that conveys deep respect can, with the right tone, convey the opposite.

役やくに立たつ (to be useful — to stand in service). 腹はらが立たつ (to get angry — your belly rises). 目め立だつ (to stand out — eyes drawn to it). 立たち場ば (たちば, standpoint). The vertical image runs through all of them — being upright means being visible, active, present. 立たつ has one of the richest idiomatic lives of any Japanese verb.

The verb itself is intransitive. ここに立たって (stand here). 椅い子すから立たつ (stand up from a chair). Its transitive partner is 立たてる (たてる) — to set something upright, to establish: 計けい画かくを立たてる (make a plan). 旗はたを立たてる (raise a flag).

The kanji 立た is a person on the ground. Two legs on a horizontal line. This picture has been stable for over three thousand years.

Particles: 立たつ takes に for where you stand — 入いり口ぐちに立たつ (stand at the entrance). For rising from somewhere, から: 席せきから立たつ (stand up from your seat).

Passive に立たたれる: indirect passive — someone standing somewhere affects you. 前まえに立たたれて見みえない (someone stood in front of me and I can't see).

Causative を／に立たたせる: making someone stand. を for coercive — 生せい徒とを廊ろう下かに立たたせる (make a student stand in the hallway — a classic Japanese school punishment).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 立てる (たてる)

Politeたちますに
Polite negたちませんに
Pastたったに

て-formたってに

パーティーに呼よぶ means “invite to a party.” 呼よぶ means “to call,” so why does it also mean “invite”? Because calling someone to a place implies you want them there. The word doesn’t draw a hard line between summoning and welcoming — whether it’s a command or a warm invitation depends on relationship and context.

呼よぶ covers calling out to someone and calling someone something. 名な前まえを呼よぶ (call someone’s name). タクシーを呼よぶ (call a taxi). 彼かれを「先せん生せい」と呼よぶ (call him “sensei”). The particle と frames the name, just like with 言いう.

The kanji 呼よ has 口くち (mouth) and 乎 (an exclamation). A mouth crying out. The original sense was exhaling forcefully. It’s the opposite of 吸す (すう, inhale), and together they form 呼こ吸きゅう (こきゅう, breathing).

Particles: 呼よぶ takes を for the person called and と for what you call them. 彼かれを「田た中なかさん」と呼よぶ (call him "Tanaka-san").

Potential が呼よべる: が marks who can be called. ここからタクシーが呼よべる (you can call a taxi from here).

Passive に呼よばれる: に marks who calls you. 先せん生せいに呼よばれた (I was called by the teacher). Very common — being summoned is a frequent experience.

Causative に…を呼よばせる: に marks who you make do the calling. 子こ供どもに友とも達だちを呼よばせる (have the kid call their friends).

In Japan, which 大だい学がく you attend often matters more for your career than what you study there. 東とう大だい (とうだい, University of Tokyo), 京きょう大だい (きょうだい, Kyoto University), 早わ稲せ田だ, 慶けい応おう — these names carry weight in job interviews decades after graduation. 大だい学がくに入はいる (enter university) is one of the defining moments of a Japanese person’s life, preceded by the infamous 受じゅ験けん (じゅけん, entrance exam) period that consumes the final year of high school.

The kanji: 大おお (big) + 学がく (study) = big study, grand learning. In the Confucian tradition, 大だい学がく was one of the Four Books — a foundational text about self-cultivation. The word carried philosophical weight long before it meant an institution with a campus and tuition.

Related words: 大だい学がく生せい (だいがくせい, university student). 大だい学がく院 (だいがくいん, graduate school). 短たん大だい (たんだい, junior college — short for 短みじか期大だい学がく). The abbreviation pattern is common: long compound → first kanji of each part.

〜やすい attached to a verb’s masu-stem means “easy to do” — one of the most useful suffixes in the language. 読よみやすい (easy to read). 分わかりやすい (easy to understand). 壊こわれやすい (fragile). This suffix is technically a different word from the adjective 安やすい, written in hiragana. But the sound-meaning bridge is real: easy, not burdensome. Learn the suffix here and you’ll use it for everything.

The adjective 安やすい itself means “cheap.” この店みせは安やすい. 安やすくて美味みしい (cheap and delicious). It’s the opposite of 高たかい in the price sense.

But the kanji 安やす tells a different story. A woman (女おんな) under a roof (宀うかんむり). The original meaning was “peace, safety.” That older sense lives on: 安あん心しん (あんしん, peace of mind), 安あん全ぜん (あんぜん, safety), 不ふ安あん (ふあん, anxiety). When 安やす is read あん in compounds, it means safety. As やすい, it means cheap.

Dictionaryやすい
Negativeやすくない
Pastやすかった
Past negやすくなかった
Adverbやすく
て-formやすくて

もっと is the everyday, colloquial "more" — the one you reach for in conversation. もっと食たべて (eat more). もっと大おおきい (bigger). もっと早はやく言いって (you should have said so sooner — literally, say it more early). It intensifies whatever follows — more of the same quality, action, or degree.

Japanese doesn't have comparative adjective forms the way English does (no "-er" suffix). Instead, you just put もっと in front of the adjective: もっと安やすい (cheaper). もっと難むずかしい (harder). Clean and consistent — no irregular comparatives, no "good/better/best" nightmares. Just もっと + any adjective.

もっと vs もう: もっと asks for a greater degree of the same thing. もう (+ a number/quantity) asks for an additional unit. もっと水みず = more water (a larger amount). もう一いち杯 = another glass (one more of the same). The difference matters: もっと adjusts the dial. もう adds to the count.

帰かえりたい — “I want to go home.” Possibly the most relatable sentence in any language. In Japanese workplaces, お先さきに失しつ礼れいします (excuse me for leaving before you) is the ritual phrase for going home, because leaving before colleagues requires an apology. 帰かえる is loaded with social weight in work culture.

帰かえる specifically means returning to where you belong. Home. Your country. Your base. This is the key distinction from 戻もどる (もどる): both mean “return,” but 帰かえる points homeward while 戻もどる means going back to any previous location. 家いえに帰かえる (go home). 席せきに戻もどる (return to your seat). You wouldn’t say 席せきに帰かえる because your seat isn’t home.

The kanji 帰かえ: the left component relates to a marching army, the right (帚) is a broom. Soldiers returning home, brooms sweeping the path for their arrival.

Particles: 帰かえる takes に for the destination — 家いえに帰かえる (go home). It also takes から for where you're returning from: 学がっ校こうから帰かえる (come home from school).

Passive に帰かえられる: indirect passive — someone going home affects you. 彼かの女じょに帰かえられた (she went home — and I was left behind / it affected me negatively). The に marks the person whose departure impacted you.

Causative を／に帰かえらせる: making or letting someone go home. を for coercive — 生せい徒とを帰かえらせる (send the student home). に for permissive — 社しゃ員に早く帰かえらせる (let the employee go home early).

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryかえるに
Negativeかえらないに
Politeかえりますに
Polite negかえりませんに
Pastかえったに
Polite pastかえりましたに
て-formかえってに
Volitionalかえろうに
Polite volかえりましょうに
Passiveかえられるに
Causativeかえらせるを／に
Caus-passかえらせられるに
Potentialかえれるに
Imperativeかえれに
Imp なさいかえりなさいに
Cond ～ばかえればに
Cond ～たらかえったらに

Most Japanese verbs let you be the one doing something. 分わかる refuses. In English, "I understand the problem" puts you in the driver's seat. In Japanese, 問もん題だいが分わかる — the problem is the subject. It's the thing that becomes clear. You're just standing there when clarity arrives. Understanding isn't an action. It's weather.

The kanji 分わ is a knife (刀かたな) splitting something in two (八はち). Dividing, distinguishing, telling apart. 分わかる is the moment confusion cracks open and you can see through it.

分わかる vs 知しる: 知しる is about possessing information — do you have this fact stored? 分わかる is about comprehension — does this click? 住じゅう所しょを知しっている (I know the address). この文ぶん法ぽうが分わかる (I understand this grammar). You 知しる a phone number. You 分わかる calculus.

分わかった is technically past tense, but in conversation it means "got it" — an acknowledgment, not a report about history. Someone explains, you say 分わかった.

Particles: 分わかる takes が — the thing understood is the grammatical subject. 日に本ほん語ごが分わかる (Japanese is understandable to me). You aren't acting on it; it's revealing itself to you.

Passive: Rarely used — 分わかる already works like an English passive. に分わかられる exists in theory but is uncommon.

Causative を／に分わからせる: making someone understand. に marks the person: 子こ供どもに分わからせる (make the child understand).

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryわかるが
Negativeわからないが
Politeわかりますが
Polite negわかりませんが
Pastわかったが
Polite pastわかりましたが
て-formわかってが
Volitionalわかろうが
Polite volわかりましょうが
Passive—
Causativeわからせるを／に
Caus-pass—
Potential—
Imperative—
Imp なさい—
Cond ～ばわかればが
Cond ～たらわかったらが

A 大おおきい room might have high ceilings — volume, overall bigness. A 広ひろい room has floor space you can move in. That's the distinction. 大おおきい measures in three dimensions. 広ひろい measures in two — spread, area, room to breathe. A warehouse is 大おおきい. A plain stretching to the horizon is 広ひろい.

広ひろい部へ屋や. 広ひろい道みち. 広ひろい心こころ (a broad mind — generosity of spirit). The word doesn't care which direction the space extends. If there's room, it's 広ひろい.

The kanji 広ひろ has the shelter radical (广まだれ) — a roof with one side open, extending outward. The verbs 広ひろがる (ひろがる, to spread out — intransitive) and 広ひろげる (ひろげる, to spread something — transitive) grow from the same root and come up constantly: 草そう原げんが広ひろがっている (the grassland stretches out). 地ち図ずを広ひろげる (unfold a map).

Dictionaryひろい
Negativeひろくない
Pastひろかった
Past negひろくなかった
Adverbひろく
て-formひろくて

数かずを数かずえる — かずをかぞえる — "count the count." Same kanji twice, different readings each time. Welcome to 数かず, a word with multiple lives.

As かず (native reading, standalone word): quantity. How many. 数かずが多おおい (there are many). 数かずが足りない (there aren't enough). As すう (Chinese-derived, in compounds): 数すう学がく (すうがく, mathematics), 数すう字じ (すうじ, numeral). And as a prefix, 数かず〜 read すう means "several" — 数すう日じつ (すうじつ, several days), 数すう回かい (すうかい, several times). Vague, small, somewhere between a few and a handful.

The kanji packs in 米こめ (rice), 女おんな (woman), and 攵のぶん (a hand with a stick). Someone tallying grains of rice. Counting as a physical, agricultural act — not abstract arithmetic.

この近ちかくにコンビニある？ — probably the first sentence you'll actually need in Japan. It uses 近ちかく, the adverb form of 近ちかい, which pulls double duty as a noun meaning "the vicinity." 駅えきの近ちかく (near the station, the area around it). Learn 近ちかく as a word in its own right, not just a conjugation.

近ちかい handles both physical and abstract proximity. 駅えきに近ちかい (close to the station). 答こたえに近ちかい (close to the answer). 近ちかい将しょう来らい (the near future). The に marks what you're close to.

The kanji 近ちか: road radical (辶しんにょう) plus 斤きん (an axe). Axe range — you have to be right next to something to swing one.

Dictionaryちかい
Negativeちかくない
Pastちかかった
Past negちかくなかった
Adverbちかく
て-formちかくて

そこ is "there" — but specifically there near you, the listener. Not far away (that's あそこ). Not here by me (that's ここ). The spot in your zone. English collapses the last two into one "there." Japanese insists on knowing whose territory the place belongs to.

そこにある (it's there, by you). そこに座ざって (sit there). In conversation, そこ also refers back to a place just mentioned: 昨日にちレストランに行いった。そこで友とも達だちに会かいった。 (Went to a restaurant yesterday. Ran into a friend there.) The restaurant enters shared conversational space, so it becomes そこ.

Say ha-shi-ru fast a few times. Three sharp syllables that feel like feet hitting pavement. Now say a-ru-ku (to walk). Slower, plodding. The sounds match the speeds.

The kanji 走はし was, in oracle bone script, a person running — full figure, arms swinging, legs mid-stride. Three thousand years of simplification compressed it, but the energy survived.

走はしる covers every kind of running. A person: 公こう園えんを走はしる. A car: 車くるまが走はしる. Pain through your body: 痛いたみが走はしる. If something is covering ground rapidly, it's 走はしる.

Particles: 走はしる takes を to mark the path — 道みちを走はしる (run along the road). This is route-marking を, not direct object を. で marks the general location: 公こう園えんで走はしる.

Passive に走はしられる: indirect passive — someone running affects you. 犬いぬに走はしられて追おいつけなかった (the dog ran off and I couldn't catch up).

Causative を／に走はしらせる: を is coercive — 生せい徒とを走はしらせる (make the students run). に is permissive.

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryはしるを
Negativeはしらないを
Politeはしりますを
Polite negはしりませんを
Pastはしったを
Polite pastはしりましたを
て-formはしってを
Volitionalはしろうを
Polite volはしりましょうを
Passiveはしられるに
Causativeはしらせるを／に
Caus-passはしらせられるに
Potentialはしれるを
Imperativeはしれを
Imp なさいはしりなさいを
Cond ～ばはしればを
Cond ～たらはしったらを

電でん気きを入はいれる — turn on the power. お茶ちゃを入はいれる — brew tea. 力ちからを入はいれる — put in effort. Three completely different English verbs, one Japanese verb. 入はいれる is "put in," and that phrase covers an astonishing range. Electricity into a circuit. Tea leaves into water. Energy into a task. Each time, something is being placed inside or activated through insertion.

The kanji 入はい: two strokes sliding downward into an opening. One of the most visually transparent characters in the language. 入はいれる is transitive — you do the putting. Its intransitive partner 入はいる (はいる) means something goes in by itself. Lock in the pair: コーヒーに砂さ糖とうを入はいれる (put sugar in coffee) vs 部へ屋やに入はいる (enter a room).

Particles: 入はいれる takes を for what you put in, に for where. 箱はこにりんごを入はいれる (put apples in the box).

Potential が入はいれられる: が marks what can be put in. まだ荷に物もつが入はいれられる (more luggage can still fit).

Passive に入はいれられる: に marks who did the putting. コーヒーに砂さ糖とうを入はいれられた (someone put sugar in my coffee — and I didn't want it).

Causative に…を入はいれさせる: に marks who you make do the inserting. 子こ供どもにお金かねを貯ちょ金きん箱ばこに入はいれさせる (make the kid put money in the piggy bank).

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 入る (はいる)

Negativeいれないを
Politeいれますを
Polite negいれませんを
Pastいれたを
Polite pastいれましたを
て-formいれてを
Volitionalいれようを
Polite volいれましょうを
Passiveいれられるに
Causativeいれさせるに／を
Caus-passいれさせられるに
Potentialいれられるが
Imperativeいれろを
Imp なさいいれなさいを
Cond ～ばいれればを
Cond ～たらいれたらを

電でん話わ番ばん号ごうを教おしえて doesn't mean "teach me your phone number." It means "tell me." That's the surprise: 教おしえる covers both teaching (sustained knowledge transfer) and simply sharing information (a quick handoff). 英えい語ごを教おしえる (teach English). 道みちを教おしえる (tell someone the way). Japanese sees both as the same act — you have knowledge, you pass it on.

The kanji 教おし: 孝 (a child learning from an elder) plus 攵のぶん (a hand with a rod). Teaching in its oldest form — an elder with a stick, guiding a younger person. The flow is always one-directional: from someone who knows to someone who doesn't.

Particles: 教おしえる takes を for what you teach and に for who. 生せい徒とに数すう学がくを教おしえる (teach students math). The person receiving knowledge takes に — they're the destination.

Potential が教おしえられる: が marks the subject that can be taught. この科か目もくが教おしえられる (this subject can be taught).

Passive に教おしえられる: に marks the teacher. 先せん生せいに大おお事ことなことを教おしえられた (I was taught something important by the teacher).

Causative に教おしえさせる: に marks who you make teach. 先せん輩ぱいに新しん人じんを教おしえさせる (have the senior teach the newcomer).

A-ru-ku. Three even syllables, each one a footfall. Step. Step. Step. Compare with 走はしる (はしる) — sharper, faster. The sounds match the paces.

The kanji 歩ある was originally two footprints alternating — left foot, right foot. Over centuries the bottom morphed into something resembling 少すく, but the walking is still there if you look.

歩あるく is walking as transportation — point A to point B on foot. 駅えきまで歩あるく (walk to the station). For aimless strolling, Japanese uses 散さん歩ぽする (さんぽする) instead. Going somewhere: 歩あるく. Wandering: 散さん歩ぽ.

Particles: 歩あるく takes を to mark the path — 道みちを歩あるく (walk along the road). Route-marking を. で for location: 公こう園えんで歩あるく.

Passive に歩あるかれる: indirect passive. 隣となりの人ひとに部へ屋やの中なかを歩あるかれて眠れなかった (the neighbor walked around in their room and I couldn't sleep).

Causative を／に歩あるかせる: を coercive — 兵へい士しを歩あるかせる (make the soldiers march). に permissive — 赤あかちゃんに歩あるかせる (let the baby walk).

Politeあるきますを

Pastあるいたを
Polite pastあるきましたを
て-formあるいてを

Polite volあるきましょうを
Passiveあるかれるに
Causativeあるかせるを／に
Caus-passあるかせられるに
Potentialあるけるを
Imperativeあるけを
Imp なさいあるきなさいを

会かいう takes に, not を. You don't "meet" someone the way you'd "hit" a ball. Meeting is convergence — you move toward a person and arrive at the same point. 友とも達だちに会かいう. The に marks the person as a destination, the same way に works with 行いく. Going to a place, going to a person — same particle, same logic.

The kanji 会かい: a lid (亼しゅう) over an opening. Things converging under one roof. The same kanji powers 会かい議ぎ (meeting), 社しゃ会かい (society), 会かい社しゃ (company).

会かいう vs 合あう: Both read あう. 会かいう is people meeting. 合あう is things matching or fitting. 友とも達だちに会かいう (meet a friend). サイズが合あう (the size fits). Different kanji, related but distinct — two things coming together in different ways.

You can also use と to emphasize mutuality: 友とも達だちと会かいう (meet with a friend — both actively participating).

Particles: 会かいう takes に for the person met. 先せん生せいに会かいう. Use と for mutual meetings: 友とも達だちと会かいう.

Passive に会かいわれる: rare in natural speech. The passive idea is usually expressed differently for this verb.

Causative を／に会かいわせる: making or letting someone meet. 二に人ひとを会かいわせる (introduce two people — make them meet).

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryあう
Negativeあわない
Politeあいます
Polite negあいません
Pastあった
Polite pastあいました
て-formあって
Volitionalあおう
Polite volあいましょう
Passiveあわれる
Causativeあわせる
Caus-passあわせられる
Potentialあえる
Imperativeあえ
Imp なさいあいなさい
Cond ～ばあえば
Cond ～たらあったら

空くう気きを読よむ was lesson 91. Now meet the other half: 書かく, the verb that turns sound into marks on a surface. Reading and writing — 読よむ and 書かく — are the pair that defines literacy.

The kanji 書か layers a hand holding a brush (聿) over 曰 (to speak). A brush that speaks — writing as speech made visible. Even though you probably type now, 書かく preserves the old connection between hand and language.

書かく covers writing and drawing, though for serious art Japanese shifts to 描く (かく or えがく). 手て紙がみを書かく (write a letter). 名な前まえを書かく (write your name). In casual usage, 書かく handles both.

Particles: 書かく takes を for what you write. 小しょう説せつを書かく. Use に for the surface and で for the instrument: 紙かみに書かく, ペンで書かく.

Potential が書かける: が — what can be written becomes the subject. 漢かん字じが書かける (I can write kanji).

Passive に書かかれる: に marks the writer. 新あたら聞きに書かかれた (it was written in the newspaper).

Causative に…を書かかせる: に marks who you make write. 生せい徒とに作さく文ぶんを書かかせる (make students write an essay).

Polite pastかきましたを
て-formかいてを
Volitionalかこうを
Polite volかきましょうを
Passiveかかれるに
Causativeかかせるに／を
Caus-passかかせられるに

Imperativeかけを
Imp なさいかきなさいを
Cond ～ばかけばを
Cond ～たらかいたらを

頭あたまがいい — smart. 頭あたまが悪い — stupid. Japanese doesn't dress it up with "bright" or "sharp" or "clever." You just rate the head directly. Good head, bad head. The equation of head with intelligence is universal, but Japanese wears it more openly than English.

The kanji 頭あたま has 頁ぺーじ on the right — a component that originally depicted a human head. You'll see it in every head-and-face kanji: 顔かお (face), 額ひたあ (forehead), 顎あご (chin). Spot 頁ぺーじ on the right side of any kanji and it's probably about something on your head.

Three readings, three lives: あたま is the everyday word for your physical head. とう is a counter for large animals — 牛うし三さん頭とう (three head of cattle). ず appears in compounds like 頭ず痛つう (headache). Same kanji, completely different contexts.

喧けん嘩かを売うる — to pick a fight. Literally: to sell a fight. You're putting a fight out there for someone to buy. And the response? 喧けん嘩かを買かう — buying the fight, accepting the challenge. The buy/sell metaphor maps perfectly onto provocation and response. You already know 買かう (to buy) — now learn its other half.

売うる is transitive — you sell something. 車くるまを売うる. 家いえを売うる. Its intransitive partner 売うれる (うれる) means something sells on its own: この本ほんはよく売うれている (this book sells well). 売うる is your decision. 売うれる is the market's response.

The kanji 売う has a display stand on top and legs walking below — something set up for people to come take away.

Particles: 売うる takes を for what you sell. 本ほんを売うる. Add に for the buyer: 友とも達だちに車くるまを売うる (sell the car to a friend).

Potential が売うれる: が — what can be sold becomes the subject. Note: 売うれる also exists as the intransitive "to sell (well)," so context matters.

Passive に売うられる: に marks the seller. 友とも達だちに売うられた can mean "was sold by a friend" — or "was betrayed."

Causative に…を売うらせる: に marks who you make sell. 店てん員いんに商しょう品ひんを売うらせる (have the clerk sell the product).

五段 Godan他動詞 — pair: 売れる (うれる)
Dictionaryうるを
Negativeうらないを
Politeうりますを
Polite negうりませんを
Pastうったを
Polite pastうりましたを
て-formうってを
Volitionalうろうを
Polite volうりましょうを
Passiveうられるに
Causativeうらせるに／を
Caus-passうらせられるに
Potentialうれるが
Imperativeうれを
Imp なさいうりなさいを
Cond ～ばうればを
Cond ～たらうったらを

大だい好すきです, said to someone's face, is a love confession. This is the word that appears at the climax of every shoujo manga, every drama confession scene. Not 愛あいしてる (あいしてる, I love you) — that exists but is almost too intense for daily use, the kind of thing married couples go years without saying. 大だい好すき is the sweet spot: deep feeling without crushing weight.

The construction is transparent: 大だい (big) + 好すき (like) = big-like. Love. Japanese intensifies feelings by putting 大だい in front: 大だい嫌きらい (hate), 大だい事じ (mportant).

For things, 大だい好すき is enthusiastic: 猫ねこが大だい好すき (I love cats). ラーメンが大だい好すき. Grammatically it works exactly like 好すき — the thing loved takes が.

Dictionaryだいすきだ
Negativeだいすきじゃない
Pastだいすきだった
Past negだいすきじゃなかった
Adverbだいすきに
て-formだいすきで

体からだに気きをつけて — take care of yourself. Literally: "pay attention to your body." This is how Japanese says goodbye when it means something — when you genuinely want the other person to stay well. The phrase reveals how Japanese thinks about health: through the body directly, not through abstract "feeling."

からだ is the body as a whole system. 体からだが強つよい (physically strong). 体からだの調ちょう子しが悪い (feeling unwell — "the body's condition is bad"). You don't "feel sick" in Japanese. Your body's condition is off.

The kanji 体からだ is a simplified form of 體, which originally had 骨 (bone) — the body understood through its skeleton. The modern form uses 本ほん (root) next to the person radical: a person's fundamental physical self. In compounds, the reading shifts to たい: 体たい力りょく (stamina), 体たい重じゅう (body weight), 体たい温おん (body temperature).

すぐそこ — "right there." Not "soon there" — right there, very close, just a few steps away. Most learners meet すぐ as a time word (immediately, right away), but it works for space too: no gap in distance, no gap in time. A straight line from here to there, from now to then.

すぐ行いく (I'll go right away). すぐ来くる (coming immediately). 駅えきのすぐ近ちかく (right near the station). The kanji 直なお means straight, direct — a line without bends. すぐ applies that directness to both time and space.

You'll rarely see the kanji written. すぐ lives in hiragana. But 直なお shows up in its other readings: 直なおす (to fix — make straight), 正しょう直じき (honest — correct and straight).

Look at 飛と. Those two wing-like strokes fanning out on the right, the compact body on the left. It's a bird in flight, caught in a single character. Even after three thousand years of simplification from its oracle bone form — where it was obviously a bird with outspread wings — the flight is still visible. Few kanji are this expressive.

飛とぶ means both "to fly" and "to jump" — any movement that leaves the ground. 鳥とりが飛とぶ (a bird flies). ページが飛とぶ (a page is skipped — it "flies" over). 噂うわさが飛とぶ (rumors spread). The common thread: something leaves its position and moves through space rapidly.

Particles: 飛とぶ takes を for the space traveled through — 空そらを飛とぶ (fly through the sky). Route-marking を. For destination: に or へ.

Passive に飛とばれる: rare. 鳥とりに飛とばれた (the bird flew away on me).

Causative: The transitive verb 飛とばす (to make fly, to skip) is used more naturally than the causative form 飛とばせる.

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 飛ばす (とばす)
Dictionaryとぶ
Negativeとばない
Politeとびます
Polite negとびません
Pastとんだ
Polite pastとびました
て-formとんで
Volitionalとぼう
Polite volとびましょう
Passiveとばれる
Causativeとばせる
Caus-passとばせられる
Potentialとべる
Imperativeとべ
Imp なさいとびなさい
Cond ～ばとべば
Cond ～たらとんだら

とてもできない — "I absolutely can't do it." Wait, doesn't とても mean "very"? It does — with positive statements. But pair it with a negative and it flips: "by no means," "not at all possible." とても間あいだに合あわない (there's no way we'll make it in time). This was actually the word's original meaning — "in any case, no matter what" — and the "very" sense came later.

In its everyday role, とても is the neutral, all-purpose intensifier. とてもおいしい (very delicious). とても大おおきい (very big). It sits before whatever it modifies. No complications.

とても vs すごく vs 非ひ常じょうに: all mean "very" at different registers. とても is neutral — works everywhere. すごく is casual, emphatic — friends gushing about food. 非ひ常じょうに (ひじょうに) is formal — news reports and papers. とても is the safe pick.

Japanese question words are shapeshifters. You learned this with いつ (when → always/someday/anytime). だれ does the same thing:

だれも → everyone (+ positive) / nobody (+ negative)
だれか → someone
だれでも → anyone

The も version is the tricky one. It flips based on the verb: だれもいる (everyone is here) vs だれもいない (nobody is here). Same word, opposite meanings, controlled entirely by positive or negative. It catches people off guard but follows the same logic as English "anybody."

The kanji 誰だれ has 言い (speech) on the left — a voice calling out, asking someone to identify themselves. The polite form is どなた: どなたですか (who might you be?) vs だれ？ (who?). The register gap is significant.

猫ねこが好すき. Not 猫ねこを好すき. The cats are the subject — they're the likeable ones. You aren't even grammatically present. This is one of the most important structural differences between English and Japanese: liking isn't framed as an action you perform. It's a quality of the thing liked. Cats are likeable. Coffee is likeable. Music is likeable. And you're the person those qualities happen to.

The kanji 好す: 女おんな (woman) and 子こ (child). A woman with a child — the image of natural affection, something precious held close.

好すき covers everything from mild preference to romantic confession. コーヒーが好すき (I like coffee). あの人ひとが好すき (I'm attracted to that person). In a confession, 好すきです alone carries the full weight of "I have feelings for you." The simplicity is what makes it powerful.

Dictionaryすきだ
Negativeすきじゃない
Pastすきだった
Past negすきじゃなかった
Adverbすきに
て-formすきで

空くう気きを読よむ — read the room. Literally: "read the air." The ability to sense unspoken mood and adjust your behavior is considered a fundamental social skill in Japan. Someone who can't is called KY (ケーワイ, short for 空くう気き読よめない). The verb went from reading text to reading people, and that jump tells you something about what Japanese culture values.

The kanji 読よ has 言い (words) on the left. Reading is receiving words. The original meaning was reading aloud — in ancient China, reading was vocal, not silent. That older sense survives in 句く読とう点てん (punctuation).

本ほんを読よむ. 新しん聞ふんを読よむ. 漢かん字じが読よめる (I can read kanji — one of the most satisfying sentences you'll eventually say).

Particles: 読よむ takes を — the text is your direct object. 本ほんを読よむ.

Potential が読よめる: が marks what can be read. 漢かん字じが読よめる.

Passive に読よまれる: に marks the reader. 日にっ記きを母ははに読よまれた (my diary was read by my mother) — classic adversative passive.

Causative に…を読よませる: に marks who you make read. 生せい徒とに本ほんを読よませる (have students read a book).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryよむ

Politeよみます
Polite negよみません
Pastよんだ
Polite pastよみました
て-formよんで
Volitionalよもう
Polite volよみましょう
Passiveよまれる
Causativeよませる
Caus-passよませられる
Potentialよめる
Imperativeよめ
Imp なさいよみなさい
Cond ～ばよめば
Cond ～たらよんだら

次つぎは何なに？ — "What's next?" A tiny word you'll use a hundred times a day. 次つぎの電でん車しゃ (the next train). 次つぎの日にち (the next day). It always needs の when modifying a noun — 次つぎ電でん車しゃ doesn't work. This marks it as a true noun, not an adjective.

In compounds, the reading shifts to じ and the meaning broadens to "order" or "secondary": 次し第だい (depends on / gradually), 目もく次じ (table of contents — literally "eye-order"). Recognize the reading switch: standalone つぎ, compound じ.

Learn this word. Then learn to almost never use it.

あなた is the textbook "you," and it's technically correct. But saying it to someone's face can sound cold, confrontational, or condescending. Japanese avoids second-person pronouns the way English avoids calling your boss by the wrong name. Use their name instead: 田た中なかさんはどう思おもいますか. Or just drop the subject: どう思おもう？

The exceptions are specific. A wife calling her husband あなた is an established term of endearment — warm, domestic, like "dear." In surveys and instructions, あなた works because there's no specific person to name. In song lyrics, it appears constantly as a poetic, longing "you."

The kanji 貴方あなた — "precious direction" — reveals just how honorific this word once was. Centuries of wear ground it down.

You "drink" pills in Japanese. 薬くすりを飲のむ (take medicine) — not "eat," not "take," but drink. If it goes down your throat, it's 飲のむ. This catches every English speaker off guard the first time.

Beyond medicine, 飲のむ is the standard verb for all drinking: 水みずを飲のむ, お茶ちゃを飲のむ, ビールを飲のむ. The kanji 飲の has the food radical on the left and 欠けつ (a person with mouth open) on the right. An open mouth near the food radical — but specifically for liquids.

お酒さけが飲のめる (I can drink alcohol) is how you say you drink — not as a question about what you're doing right now, but about capability and willingness. 飲のめる？ directed at someone is a common shorthand for "do you drink?"

Particles: 飲のむ takes を — the liquid is your direct object. ジュースを飲のむ.

Potential が飲のめる: が marks what can be drunk. この水みずが飲のめる (this water is drinkable).

Passive に飲のまれる: に marks the drinker. 最さい後ごの一いっ本ぽんを弟おとうとに飲のまれた (my little brother drank the last one on me).

Causative に…を飲のませる: に marks who you make drink. 子こ供どもに薬くすりを飲のませる (make the child take medicine).

Never call a person 古ふるい. The word is for things only — old buildings, old books, old traditions. An old person? Completely different vocabulary. Calling someone 古ふるい means they're outdated, behind the times. その考かんがえ方かたは古ふるい (that way of thinking is old-fashioned). It's a criticism, not a description of age.

With things, 古ふるい pairs with 新あたらしい (new). And it can carry warmth: 古ふるい町まち並ならみ (old townscape) evokes charm. 古ふるい友とも達だち (old friend) means a friend you've known long — not a friend who's aging.

The kanji 古ふる: 十じゅう (ten) over 口くち (mouth). Ten generations of mouths passing down stories. That which has been spoken of for ages.

Dictionaryふるい
Negativeふるくない
Pastふるかった
Past negふるくなかった
Adverbふるく
て-formふるくて

質しつ問もんがある — "I have a question." This is how you raise your hand in class, interrupt a presentation, or formally request information. 質しつ問もん is a deliberate, structured question — not idle wondering, not a quick aside.

質しつ問もんする vs 聞きく (to ask): 聞きく is casual, woven into conversation. 質しつ問もんする is raising your hand. ちょっと聞きいていい？ (can I ask something?) — friends talking. 質しつ問もんしてもいいですか (may I ask a question?) — meeting etiquette.

The kanji 問もん is a mouth (口くち) inside a gate (門もん) — a voice at the gate, calling out to ask for entry. It shows up in 問もん題だい (problem), 疑ぎ問もん (doubt), 訪ほう問もん (visit).

今いま (now) + 日にち (day) = today. The logic is transparent. The reading きょう has nothing to do with いま + ひ. It's a native Japanese word mapped onto Chinese characters. The regular compound reading こんにち also exists — you hear it daily in こんにちは, which literally means "as for this day."

きょう is a time word, so it needs no particle: 今日きょうは暑あつい (it's hot today). 今日きょう、学がっ校こうに行いく (I'm going to school today). No に required.

The time trio — 昨日きのう , 今日きょう , 明日あした  — all have irregular readings. The kanji say one thing, the pronunciation says another. These are among the oldest, most-used words in the language, and ancient native readings stuck even after kanji arrived. Learn them as units.

You don't "make" friends in Japanese. Friends happen. 友とも達だちができた — literally, "a friend came into existence." できる means something formed, emerged, materialized. Japanese frames friendship as something that develops naturally, not something you manufacture. The grammar encodes a different philosophy of human connection.

The kanji 友とも is two hands reaching in the same direction — people working together, aligned in purpose. In oracle bone script, it was two right hands side by side. The kanji 達たち means "to reach, to arrive." Together: your fellow-reachers, the people alongside you.

友とも達だち is the standard, default word for friend. 友とも達だちに会かいう (meet a friend). The word neighborhood: 親しん友ゆう (close friend), 知しり合あい (acquaintance), 仲なか間ま (comrade). Japanese people can be particular about who qualifies as 友とも達だち vs merely 知しり合あい.

Two different kanji, same pronunciation, overlapping meanings — the classic はやい trap. 早はやい is about timing: early, soon, premature. 速はやい is about speed: rapid, swift. 朝あさが早はやい (morning is early). 車くるまが速はやい (the car is fast). In casual writing, the distinction blurs and 早はやい covers both. But when precision matters, the split is real.

The kanji 早はや: a sun (日にち) rising above a horizon. First light. Early morning. The jump from "early" to "fast" is intuitive — what arrives early moved quickly.

The compound 早さっ速そく (immediately) combines both kanji — early + speed — and sounds decisive in conversation: 早さっ速そく始はじめましょう (let's get started right away).

Dictionaryはやい
Negativeはやくない
Pastはやかった
Past negはやくなかった
Adverbはやく
て-formはやくて

どれ is "which one?" — but only when there are three or more options. For two options, use どっち (casual) or どちら (polite). This two-vs-three split doesn't exist in English and it trips people up early. コーヒーとお茶ちゃ、どっち？ (coffee or tea?) — two choices. どれがいい？ (which one do you want?) — three or more laid out.

どれ is the question member of the ko-so-a-do thing series: これ (this), それ (that near you), あれ (that over there), どれ (which?). Before a noun, use どの instead: どの色いろ？ (which color?).

Shapeshifting with particles, same as always: どれも (all of them / none + negative), どれか (one of them), どれでも (any of them).
